serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Turmeric Fried Squid is approximately 150 g, representing a standard side-dish portion as part of a Malay rice meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175168) and cooked analogues; supplemented with Singapore HPB Foodo database for Malay stir-fried seafood dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sotong Goreng Kunyit exists in a single approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central squid (raw and cooked) entries and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried seafood analogues. Cooking oil absorption (~3–4 g fat per 100 g) and turmeric/vegetable additions are factored into the final composite. Input AI-generated values were reviewed against these approved sources and found broadly consistent; values retained with medium confidence. The input source 'Malaysian Journal of Nutrition / nutriweb.org.my' is not on the approved list and has been replaced.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood stir-fry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sotong Goreng Kunyit. GI estimated at 20 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood stir-fry dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Carbohydrate content is 4.5 g per 100 g; glycemic load per 150 g serving = (20 × 6.75) / 100 = 1.35, rounded to 1.4. This is consistent with the negligible glycemic impact expected from a predominantly protein-and-vegetable dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg per 100 g; potassium 280 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 320 mg per 100 g (480 mg per 150 g serving) represents a moderate sodium load; individuals with hypertension should limit added salt during cooking and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the full meal, while the potassium content (280 mg per 100 g) provides a partial counterbalancing benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~20)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 4.5 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of approximately 20, Turmeric Fried Squid has a negligible glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 1.4) and is well-suited for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided accompanying rice portions are controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Squid cholesterol content (~185 mg per 100 g) offset by low saturated fat and anti-inflammatory turmeric
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in cholesterol (~185 mg per 100 g) but low in saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g); current cardiovascular guidelines prioritise saturated fat reduction over dietary cholesterol restriction, and the anti-inflammatory curcumin in turmeric may confer additional cardiovascular benefit, making moderate consumption acceptable for most individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) anti-inflammatory properties; high-quality squid protein
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The curcumin in fresh turmeric has documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; squid provides high-quality complete protein to support muscle maintenance, though individuals with seafood sensitivities should exercise caution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turmeric Fried Squid (Malay: Sotong Goreng Kunyit) is a traditional Malay stir-fry dish in which fresh squid rings are flash-fried in a wok with fresh turmeric, onions, red chillies, and crunchy vegetables such as long beans or carrots. The dish is characterised by its vivid golden-yellow colour imparted by fresh turmeric, its aromatic earthiness, and the prized 'wok hei' (breath of the wok) achieved through high-heat cooking. The squid is cooked briefly to preserve a tender, juicy texture rather than becoming rubbery. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood side dish providing approximately 145 kcal, 13.5 g protein, and 8.2 g fat per 100 g. It is rich in selenium (38 mcg), vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg), phosphorus (185 mg), and riboflavin (0.35 mg), reflecting the nutritional profile of squid. The low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) and estimated glycemic index of approximately 20 make it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium at 320 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a side dish in Malay rice meals (Nasi Campur) across Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia, and is popular at both home and hawker settings for lunch and dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sotong Goreng Kunyit?
Is Sotong Goreng Kunyit suitable for diabetics?
What nutrients does fried squid with turmeric contain?
What is the glycemic index of turmeric fried squid?
Can I eat Sotong Goreng Kunyit if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: Protein side for lunch or dinner

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains an exact entry for Sotong Goreng Kunyit as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central squid entries and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried seafood analogues. The input AI-generated values were found broadly consistent with these approved-source analogues and have been retained with adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central (squid, mixed species, raw/cooked, FDC ID 175168) and HPB Singapore Foodo stir-fried seafood analogues; no exact approved-source entry for Sotong Goreng Kunyit exists. 2. The input source 'Malaysian Journal of Nutrition / nutriweb.org.my' is not on the Project Veritas approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore Foodo. 3. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for this specific stir-fried preparation not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-16. 4. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 as squid and vegetable oil used in stir-frying contain negligible trans fats. 5. Saturated fat (1.4 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.1 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.8 g) estimated from USDA squid fatty acid profile plus cooking oil contribution. 6. Cholesterol estimated at 185 mg per 100 g based on USDA squid data; squid is naturally high in cholesterol. 7. GI estimated at 20 (low) as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database high-protein seafood stir-fry analogues; glycemic load per 150 g serving ≈ 1.4. 8. Dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'vegan' present in the input have been removed as the dish contains squid (animal protein). 9. All core macros (protein_g 13.5, fat_g 8.2, carbohydrate_g 4.5) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a stir-fried squid dish; no zero-macro justification required.
